item.title: BACKTRACKS | Hope through music | A glimpse into Mereseini’s musical journey
item.description: For Fiji Corrections Service band member Mereseini Sorovaki, music has always been a gift that has shaped her life, opened doors she never imagined and, today, is helping others find hope during some of the most difficult periods of their lives. The 30-year-old, who is originally from Nakasaleka in Kadavu and shares maternal links to Bua, believes music has the power to heal, inspire and rehabilit
item.content:encoded:
For Fiji Corrections Service band member Mereseini Sorovaki, music has always been a gift that has shaped her life, opened doors she never imagined and, today, is helping others find hope during some of the most difficult periods of their lives.

The 30-year-old, who is originally from Nakasaleka in Kadavu and shares maternal links to Bua, believes music has the power to heal, inspire and rehabilitate.

Working with the Fiji Corrections Service has strengthened that belief.

“Being part of the Fiji Corrections Service has really opened my eyes,” she said.

“I’ve realised that many of the people who come through here have been through a lot in life.

“Sometimes we don’t know what they’ve experienced before they ended up here.”

Despite the challenges inmates face, she said one thing never failed to move her.

“Whenever we have church services, you see them singing with all their hearts. It feels as though a huge burden has been lifted off their shoulders through music.”

Those moments have convinced her that rehabilitation is possible.

“That’s when you realise some people make mistakes because they’re trying to escape something else in their lives. That’s why I believe everyone deserves a second chance.”

Sorovaki now uses music to encourage young offenders to express themselves positively.

“We’ve taught some of the younger ones to use music as an outlet instead of turning to other things.”

One experience remains especially close to her heart.

“We taught two inmates while they were here, and after they were released, they joined the army Band.

Knowing they found a positive path through music makes me proud because it feels like I’ve been able to make a difference in someone’s life.”

For Sorovaki, her own musical journey began long before she joined the Corrections Service.

She grew up in Delainavesi, Lami, where life was simple and centred around family and church.

“I grew up like many other girls. I stayed at home most of the time and was treasured by my family. I wasn’t exposed to much compared with what I experience today.”

Her earliest memories of singing was at church.

“Everything started in church. I sang in Sunday school and during church services. When I was about five years old, I realised I had a voice.”

As a child, she and her cousins would often spend their days together playing games and telling stories.

One afternoon would change how she viewed her talent forever.

“My cousins made us watch Sister Act, and after the movie they wanted us to act out the singing scenes. I was the youngest, but they gave me the main solo from ‘Oh Happy Day’.”

She still remembers the feeling after finishing the song.

“That was the moment I knew I could really sing.”

Although she recognised her ability early, confidence did not come easily.

In 2011, while she was in Year 11, her mother encouraged her to audition for the Kaila! Star quest competition during the Hibiscus Festival.

“I was a very shy person then. I preferred singing around my friends and wasn’t ready to perform in public.”

At her mother’s insistence, she auditioned.

“I made it into the Top 10 finalists, but because I was so shy, I never really told anyone.”

item.title: TOURISM TALANOA | Tourism cannot be the only safety net
item.description: EVERY national budget tells us something about where the country is at, what Government is worried about and where the pressure points are likely to fall. The 2026/2027 National Budget, framed as “responsible” and “sustainable,” is but a reflection of Fiji’s difficult fiscal reality.
item.content:encoded:
EVERY national budget tells us something about where the country is at, what Government is worried about and where the pressure points are likely to fall.

The 2026/2027 National Budget, framed as “responsible” and “sustainable,” is but a reflection of Fiji’s difficult fiscal reality.

Public debt remains high, government expenditure is under constant pressure, and fuel price volatility has once again exposed how vulnerable small island economies are to global shocks, with the difficult truth we must face being that as usual, Fiji was ill-prepared for any shock.

Every sector is being asked to do more with less, and the belt-tightening being called for within the public sector is an exercise in futility given there has been no historical practice to provide much needed muscle memory.

The measures proposed are too short-sighted—focused on immediate fixes rather than building the resilience, productivity and economic lift Fiji urgently needs.

Once again, the industry’s central role in the economy was recognised, with Government noting that tourism contributes around 40 per cent to Fiji’s economic activity through employment, foreign exchange earnings, investment, aviation demand, agriculture linkages, transport, retail, construction, cultural industries, marine services and thousands of small businesses whose businesses are tied to visitor confidence.

It was also acknowledged that tourism must be protected because without support, industries do not grow.

Noting that we need more rooms, better infrastructure, stronger airport capacity, expanded tourism products and a safe, secure destination experience.

We have been saying exactly this for many years, usually while in queues waiting for water connections, caught in traffic due to delayed road repairs, addressing power outages that demand back-up diesel generator usage, pleading with Immigration for recruitment gap relief, and weekly follow ups to regulatory agencies sitting on approvals, permits and licenses.

There are some useful measures provided that include the extension of diesel duty concessions to hotels using diesel for power generation from July 1 to October 31, 2026 that will no doubt provide some relief for properties that rely on backup generation or operate in areas where energy reliability remains a daily operational issue.

It will not solve the deeper utility challenge, but it recognises the cost pressure. Then after six years of consistently requesting, we finally saw the amendment to the Time of Supply rule in the VAT act come through.

There’s also the extended support around fuel and energy cost volatility more broadly, including concessions for Energy Fiji Limited and bus operators, and targeted assistance for vulnerable households and very small businesses.

These are sensible interventions as fuel shocks do not stay neatly inside one sector. They travel through freight, food prices, electricity, transport, airfares and household spending.

Tourism feels this early because our product is built on movement, logistics and service delivery across islands, roads, boats, buses, flights and supply chains, while being highly reliant on imports.

There are also some confidence-building measures that matter to all businesses. Any attempt to reduce employment costs, support private sector activity and improve ease of doing business is welcomed more broadly across industries.

Tourism operators need to constantly reinvest — to refurbish rooms, expand products, train staff, source more local produce, improve sustainability and support the communities around them.

But investment confidence depends on predictability so businesses can plan around known costs. It struggles when major fiscal changes arrive suddenly, with unclear application and little time to adjust.

The biggest concern for tourism in this Budget is the proposed five per cent Tourism Services Tax, to be applied from September 1, 2026 to hotels and tour operators with annual turnover above $2 million, with revenue directed to support Fiji Airways.

We all recognise the airline’s vital role in Fiji’s connectivity, visitor arrivals, trade, employment and national brand, and we understand its exposure to fuel volatility, debt servicing, foreign exchange movements and global competition.

Without reliable air access, the wider economy would suffer quickly.

But this measure is too short-sighted. It places the burden on one sector already under pressure, erodes reinvestment capacity and risks undermining the very resilience and competitiveness that Fiji needs.

This is not an argument against Fiji Airways—it is an argument for fairness, transparency, and a properly designed national response.

With a valid concern that while marginalising other businesses, there may be little real impact where it is expected and a longer term negative impact on the destination’s appeal.

Several other support measures have been outlined for the airline. The extension of the loss carry-forward period from 8 years to 15 years for operating international services and the extension of the moratorium on revised outdoor fees to July 2027, which is expected to provide relief of around $10 million annually.

An additional $200 million guarantee is also under consideration.

Those are significant measures that rightly reflect the strategic importance of the airline. Which is precisely the point. If Fiji Airways is a national strategic asset, then support for it must be treated as a national issue, not placed disproportionately on tourism operators through a sector-specific turnover tax.

A turnover tax is a blunt instrument applied whether a business is profitable or not. It does not care whether an operator is carrying COVID-era debt, managing thin margins, operating seasonally, facing labour shortages, paying higher wages, maintaining ageing infrastructure, absorbing utility costs or meeting the growing costs of compliance across tax, health, OHS, liquor, fire, environment and labour requirements.

It certainly would not care if fuel prices went up again, the already recognised super El Nino season brought drastic weather with it, or some other reason wrought havoc on an industry that must consistently fight to stay competitive.

It also does not recognise the commercial structure of tourism. Many hotels and tour operators have already contracted rates, accepted deposits, confirmed group bookings and entered wholesale arrangements well beyond September 1, 2026 and 2027-2028 contracts have either just been signed or are on the verge of confirmation. These are binding, commercial commitments and contracts.

Beyond the immediate price impact, the proposed tax risks damaging confidence in Fiji as a reliable destination for contracting and selling.

item.title: Women and the economy-WEE-NAP goals, WEBC role, economic contribution
item.description: WHEN we talk about growing Fiji’s economy, we often focus on investment, productivity, exports, infrastructure and labour market participation. Yet one of our country’s greatest opportunities lies much closer to home: supporting more women to start, grow, formalise and scale successful businesses. Across our urban centres, rural communities, creative industries, service sectors, agricultural value
item.content:encoded:
WHEN we talk about growing Fiji’s economy, we often focus on investment, productivity, exports, infrastructure and labour market participation. Yet one of our country’s greatest opportunities lies much closer to home: supporting more women to start, grow, formalise and scale successful businesses.

Across our urban centres, rural communities, creative industries, service sectors, agricultural value chains and informal economy, women entrepreneurs are creating jobs, supporting families, strengthening communities and driving innovation.

However, despite their contributions, many continue to face structural barriers that limit their ability to fully realise their business potential.

The Fiji Women’s Economic Empowerment National Action Plan (WEE-NAP) 2025–2030 recognises both the scale of women’s contribution and the scale of the opportunity before us with a plan to increase:

1) women’s labour force participation from 32 per cent to 60 per cent by 2030,

2) proportion of women-owned registered micro, small and medium enterprises from 19 per cent to 50 per cent by 2029.

Research consistently demonstrates that entrepreneurs who have access to mentors are more likely to grow their businesses, increase revenues, build confidence, strengthen decision-making and develop the professional networks needed to succeed.

Effective mentorship offers guidance, accountability, access to experience and perhaps most importantly, the confidence to pursue opportunities that may otherwise seem out of reach.

The WEE-NAP specifically identifies targeted mentorship as a key intervention under Pillar Two: Women in Business, recognising that access to capability development, networks, leadership support and business guidance are essential components of inclusive economic growth. However, this only forms part of the extensive workplan outlined under WEE-NAP.

At the Fiji Commerce & Employers Federation, we have long understood that business success is strengthened through collaboration, shared experience, and leadership development. Through the Women Entrepreneurs Business Council (WEBC), we have seen firsthand the impact that peer learning, mentoring relationships and practical business support can have on women entrepreneurs at every stage of their business journey.

This understanding has informed the development and execution of the EmpowerHer Pathways: Inclusive Mentorship and Capacity Building for Women-Led Businesses initiative with the support of Ministry of Women, Children and Social Protection (MWCSP).

The program has been specifically designed to move beyond traditional classroom training by placing mentorship at its core.

Participants worked directly with experienced women business leaders to develop practical business improvement plans, strengthen their communication and leadership skills, and build lasting professional networks that continue beyond the formal training programme.

Particular emphasis is placed on supporting women entrepreneurs operating in the informal economy, women with disabilities, those engaged in creative and cultural industries, and women seeking to strengthen their access to business development opportunities and formal markets.

So far, WEBC have completed two sessions across Suva and Nadi with 50 mentees and 15 mentors!

The value of initiatives such as these extends well beyond the individual entrepreneur.

Stronger women-owned businesses contribute to greater household resilience, increased employment opportunities, more diverse industries, stronger local economies and enhanced national productivity.

For Fiji to achieve truly inclusive and sustainable economic growth, we must also invest in people. Mentorship, capability development, leadership support and access to professional networks must be strategic investments in our nation’s economic future.

As Fiji continues to pursue its development ambitions under Vision 2050, strengthening pathways for women entrepreneurs to succeed will remain essential.

By creating opportunities for women to learn, lead, connect, formalise and grow, we are investing in a stronger, more resilient, more productive and more inclusive economy for all.

n GAYLENE KAMALI is manager Advocacy & Corporate Communication for Fiji Commerce and Employers Federation (FCEF). The views expressed in this article are not necessarily the views of The Fiji Times.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: The silent epidemic | Exploring Fiji’s hidden eye health crisis
item.description: Somewhere in rural Fiji right now, a grandmother is going blind from a cataract that a 20-minute operation could fix. A few villages over, a man with diabetes is losing his sight to a disease he doesn’t know he has. Neither of them is unusual. Across Fiji, the biggest barrier standing between people and clear vision often isn’t the cost of treatment or the distance to a hospital — it’s simply not
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere in rural Fiji right now, a grandmother is going blind from a cataract that a 20-minute operation could fix. A few villages over, a man with diabetes is losing his sight to a disease he doesn’t know he has. Neither of them is unusual. Across Fiji, the biggest barrier standing between people and clear vision often isn’t the cost of treatment or the distance to a hospital — it’s simply not knowing help exists, or not believing it’s worth seeking.

That gap between treatable and treated is what eye health workers in Fiji are increasingly trying to close, and the numbers explain why the effort matters.

A problem hiding behind “just getting old”

Globally, roughly nine in ten people living with blindness or vision loss didn’t have to be — their condition could have been prevented or treated if caught early enough. Fiji’s own research tells a similar story. A population-based survey of adults on Fiji’s main island found that more than half of people with a current vision problem — 53 per cent — had never sought treatment for it. When researchers asked why, the leading reason wasn’t money or distance, though those mattered too. It was attitude: 57 per cent simply accepted their failing sight as something to live with, something they “could manage.” Cost was cited by just 12 per cent, fear of treatment by 8 per cent. (Source: WHO Western Pacific data, Fiji-specific population health studies, and Fred Hollows Foundation reporting)

A separate household survey in Fiji’s Central Division found that while 86 per cent of people knew at least one place to get their eyes checked, only 66 per cent of those with an actual eye problem had ever gone. Older Fijians were the least likely of all to seek care — precisely the group most at risk of cataract, glaucoma and diabetes-related vision loss.

Diabetes is turning Fiji’s eye health into an emergency

Fiji’s eye problems are tangled up with one of its biggest public health challenges: diabetes. The Pacific region has some of the highest diabetes rates on Earth, and Fiji is no exception — research on Fijians aged 40 and over found a diabetes prevalence of around 41 per cent once adjusted for age, ethnicity and gender, and an estimated 60 per cent of those cases go undiagnosed, particularly in rural areas where testing is harder to access.

That matters for eyesight because diabetic retinopathy — damage to the blood vessels at the back of the eye — has become the second-leading cause of vision loss in Fiji after cataract. A study of patients newly referred to diabetic eye screening in Fiji found that more than a quarter already had sight-threatening retinopathy by the time they were checked, and nearly 7 per cent already had some degree of vision impairment from it. The disease is especially dangerous because it creeps up silently; people often feel fine until the damage is advanced and much harder to reverse.

Avoidable vision loss isn’t only an adult or elderly problem. A nationwide study of Fijian children found that roughly three in ten cases of childhood vision loss stemmed from causes that could have been prevented or treated, rather than from conditions present at birth. Left unaddressed, childhood vision problems don’t just affect reading and schoolwork — research elsewhere in the world has linked uncorrected vision loss in children to lower school performance and reduced earning potential decades later.

The encouraging part of this story is that Fiji isn’t standing still. The Pacific Eye Institute, based in Suva, has been running surgical outreach trips to underserved areas — a recent mission to Ba, run with Aspen Medical Ba Hospital and Lautoka Hospital, was specifically aimed at clearing a backlog of patients in Fiji’s Western Division. Alongside the surgery, the team has been training local nurses and primary health workers to spot eye problems early, rather than relying solely on specialists who are concentrated in a handful of hospitals.

One particularly promising approach tackles the diabetes-eye connection directly: rather than relying only on busy clinical nurses, programs backed by the Fred Hollows Foundation have begun training community health workers — the people already trusted and present in villages — to talk to residents about diabetic eye disease and refer them for screening. It’s a low-cost way of reaching people long before their vision is in serious danger.

Fiji has also had a genuine public health win worth highlighting: trachoma, a bacterial infection that remains one of the world’s leading infectious causes of blindness, was found in a survey of the Western Division to no longer meet the World Health Organization’s threshold for being a public health problem there — a reminder that sustained screening and hygiene programs can actually turn the tide on a blinding disease.

There’s also a hard economic argument for funding this kind of work. Research commissioned by the Fred Hollows Foundation and Victoria University found that every dollar spent on cataract surgery generates, on average, more than $20 back into the local economy — through people returning to work, caring for family, and no longer needing support themselves. For a country like Fiji, where eye care budgets compete with many other health priorities, that’s a difficult figure to ignore.

None of this works, though, if people don’t know the services exist — or don’t believe their failing eyesight is worth mentioning to anyone. That’s really the heart of the matter: Fiji has surgeons who can remove a cataract in minutes, screening tools that can catch diabetic damage before it’s permanent, and outreach teams willing to travel to remote islands. What’s still catching up is the everyday awareness that vision loss, in the vast majority of cases, doesn’t have to be accepted as fate. A free eye check, a five-minute conversation with a village health worker, or simply telling family that your sight has changed — these small, ordinary acts may end up doing more to prevent blindness in Fiji than any single piece of medical equipment.

Bikash Gyawali is a consultant optometrist at Asgar Optometry.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: National Insurance Awareness Day | Insurance Is More Than a Policy | It Is a Promise That Helps Build Fiji’s Future
item.description: NONE of us expects the phone call that changes everything. A family home damaged by a cyclone. A business destroyed by fire. A serious road accident. An unexpected illness.
item.content:encoded:
NONE of us expects the phone call that changes everything.

A family home damaged by a cyclone. A business destroyed by fire. A serious road accident. An unexpected illness.

These moments arrive without warning. When they do, insurance becomes far more than a policy document or a premium payment. It becomes reassurance, financial security and, most importantly, the confidence to begin again.

For almost 39 years, TISA Insurance has had the privilege of protecting the people and businesses of Fiji. We have celebrated their successes, stood beside them through their greatest challenges and helped thousands recover and rebuild after life’s unexpected events.

That is why Insurance Awareness Day matters. It reminds us that while we cannot prevent uncertainty, we can prepare for it.

Here in Fiji, resilience is part of who we are. Our islands have taught us the importance of standing together through adversity. Whether recovering from cyclones, rebuilding after fires or supporting loved ones through difficult times, our greatest strength has always been our communities.

Insurance reflects those same values. It is a promise that no one should have to face hardship alone.

Behind every insurance policy is a person. A young couple buying their first home. A farmer protecting years of hard work. A small business owner investing in a dream. A taxi driver supporting a family. An employer creating opportunities for their staff.

Every policy represents someone’s aspirations, livelihood and future. We are not simply protecting assets; we are protecting the lives and ambitions that depend on them.

Insurance also plays a vital role in Fiji’s economy. It gives families the confidence to invest, businesses the certainty to grow and financial institutions the assurance to lend. It helps communities recover faster after disasters and supports long-term economic resilience.

A resilient insurance industry helps build a resilient Fiji.

Over nearly four decades, our organisation has evolved alongside the communities we serve, first as Dominion Insurance, then as Capital Insurance and today as TISA Insurance. While our name has changed, our purpose has remained the same: to stand beside our customers when they need us most.

Throughout our journey, we have witnessed remarkable stories of courage and resilience. Families rebuilding after cyclones. Businesses reopening after devastating losses. Communities recovering from events that once seemed overwhelming.

These experiences have reinforced an important truth: insurance is not about expecting the worst. It is about believing in tomorrow.

Trust lies at the heart of everything we do. It is not built through advertising or a familiar logo. It is earned through the promises we keep, the integrity with which we operate and the compassion we show when our customers need us most.

Our commitment extends well beyond insurance. Across Fiji, we proudly support initiatives that strengthen communities through sport, youth development, women’s empowerment, entrepreneurship, education and leadership. We believe stronger communities build a stronger nation.

Looking ahead, the role of insurance will become even more important. Climate change, evolving technology and emerging risks are reshaping our world, requiring insurers to continue delivering innovative solutions, outstanding service and protection that keeps pace with the changing needs of Fiji.

As we mark Insurance Awareness Day, we encourage every family and every business to review the protection they have in place. Insurance is not about expecting something to go wrong. It is about having the confidence to pursue your ambitions, knowing that if the unexpected happens, you will not face it alone.

For almost 39 years, that belief has guided our organisation and continues to guide us today.

Our name may have evolved, but our purpose remains unchanged.

To help build a stronger, more resilient Fiji

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: National Insurance Awareness Day | Life Insurance: Protecting dreams, securing the future
item.description: I am your safeguard, your lasting flame. A beautiful line written by somebody clearly depicts the true picture of life and life insurance.
item.content:encoded:
I am your safeguard, your lasting flame.

A beautiful line written by somebody clearly depicts the true picture of life and life insurance.

Life Insurance so important, yet the least bothered financial instrument.

Life insurance is one of the most important financial instruments available today. It provides protection, security, and peace of mind by ensuring that the financial needs of a family or business are met even in the event of unforeseen circumstances.

Life insurance is considered a must-have because it directly protects families, businesses, and financial goals against uncertainty.

At its core, life insurance is a contract that guarantees financial support to beneficiaries upon the death of the policyholder.

This makes it a vital tool for income replacement, helping dependents maintain their standard of living.

It also safeguards against liabilities such as loans, mortgages, and other debts, ensuring that loved ones are not burdened financially.

Beyond protection, life insurance policies are also considered assets.

Endowment, money-back, and whole-life plans combine insurance with savings, offering maturity benefits and wealth creation opportunities.

These policies can be pledged as collateral, withdrawn for emergencies, or used to supplement retirement income. Thus, life insurance serves both as a shield and a financial resource.

For businesses, specialized policies such as Keyman Insurance and Group Insurance provide continuity and stability.

They protect companies against the loss of critical employees and strengthen employer-employee benefit structures.

Life insurance policies provide various optional add on benefits like Accident Benefit,

Disability Benefit, Critical Illness Benefit, Funeral Benefit and Waiver of Premium Benefits under different plans.

In case of need, life insurance companies give loan to the customers under their policies.

Life Insurance Policies can be assigned (mortgaged) to raise loan from other financial institutions.

Life insurance policies are available for all ages, for different terms and all kinds of needs of the prospective customers.

There are children’s policies which help in providing for their future expenses like funding their tertiary education or starting a business.

There are policies known as “Money Back” or “Anticipated Endowment” which provide for a payment of a part of the target Sum Assured (say, 10/15/20/25%) at regular intervals of 3/4/5 years during the policy term.

The risk cover on the life of the policy holder continues for the full sum assured throughout the term.

Policies that cover the risk on the life of the policy holder “as long as he/she is alive” are available which are known as “Whole Life” policies.

To invest a lumpsum amount, “Single Premium” plans are available. Under these policies, the premium is to be paid only once.

This is a very good investment option delivering far better returns than other comparable options.

Needless to add, all the usual benefits of life insurance policies mentioned above are very much available.

In fact, every person must invest in a life insurance policy of appropriate sum assured and premium to address the hazards of living mentioned above. As we say, “Life Insurance is all about Loving and Caring”!

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: LEADERSHIP FIJI | People-centered healthcare
item.description: Leadership was never part of the original plan for Dr Rachel Devi. As a secondary school student in Suva, medicine was always the goal, but she envisioned herself becoming a cardiologist, treating patients one at a time.
item.content:encoded:
Leadership was never part of the original plan for Dr Rachel Devi.

As a secondary school student in Suva, medicine was always the goal, but she envisioned herself becoming a cardiologist, treating patients one at a time.

Today, as Head of the Family Health Unit at Fiji’s Ministry of Health and Medical Services, her work reaches far beyond the walls of a consulting room, shaping policies and programmes that affect the health and wellbeing of thousands of Fijians.

It is a journey that has transformed not only her career, but also her understanding of what leadership truly means.

“My passion for medicine goes back to my high school days,” she said.

“Initially, I wanted to become a cardiologist rather than work in public health.”

That ambition changed after she was posted to HIV medicine early in her career.

“It was during that time that I realised the profound impact a doctor working in public health could have on an entire population. That experience changed the direction of my career.”

From HIV medicine she moved into sexual and reproductive health before eventually leading the Family Health Unit, where she now oversees national programmes focused on maternal, child and reproductive health.

For Dr Devi, the greatest attraction of public health lies in its ability to improve lives on a national scale.

“When I look at population health, clinicians do remarkable work caring for individual patients in hospitals,” she said.

“However, as a public health physician, our work has the potential to improve the lives of entire communities. Instead of helping one patient at a time, we can positively influence the health of all women, children and young people across the country.”

That broader perspective also means confronting some of Fiji’s most pressing public health challenges.

While acknowledging progress across many areas, she believes the country’s burden of non-communicable diseases remains one of its greatest concerns.

“NCDs affect a significant proportion of our population and have enormous economic consequences, with hundreds of millions of dollars lost annually,.

“We are also seeing major challenges surrounding HIV, particularly among young people, as well as the growing impact of drug use.”

Although serious, she remains optimistic.

“These are significant issues, but they are not insurmountable. With strong prevention and mitigation strategies, we can address them effectively.”

Behind those national responsibilities lies another lesson she considers equally important: leadership itself is a skill that must be learned.

A graduate of Leadership Fiji, Dr Devi credits the programme with helping prepare her for responsibilities that medical school alone could not.

“Honestly, we’re not born leaders. As medical doctors, we’re trained in medicine, not necessarily in leadership. Yet as we take on greater responsibility, leadership becomes an essential part of our role.”

Leadership Fiji, she said, equipped her with practical tools she continues to apply in managing teams and national health programs.

“These are essential competencies for anyone leading a team, a programme or even a country.”

Another significant influence came through the Fiji Australia Awards Alumni Reflective Leadership Programme, which introduced her to the concept of reflective leadership.

Rather than simply focusing on outcomes, reflective leadership encourages leaders to regularly examine their own performance, strengths and weaknesses.

“It is about taking the time to step back and ask yourself important questions: What am I doing? Am I doing it well? What can I improve?” she said.

She believes many leaders fail to create enough space for reflection, despite its importance in continuous improvement.

After serving at the Ministry of Health headquarters before undertaking postgraduate study at the University of Sydney, she found that returning with fresh perspectives strengthened both her confidence and effectiveness.

“The programme reinforced the importance of continually reflecting, re-energising yourself, strengthening areas of weakness and continuing to grow as a leader.”

No experience, however, tested those leadership lessons more than the COVID-19 pandemic.

item.title: FOCUS I Coalitions, comprimise and collapse
item.description: IT was, by any measure, an improbable alliance. In June 1992, Mahendra Chaudhry, the man whose Finance Ministry Rabuka had been snatched at gunpoint five years earlier during the 1987 military coups — agreed to install his erstwhile tormentor as Prime Minister of Fiji. The arrangement was born not of goodwill but of pure arithmetic.
item.content:encoded:
IT was, by any measure, an improbable alliance. In June 1992, Mahendra Chaudhry, the man whose Finance Ministry Rabuka had been snatched at gunpoint five years earlier during the 1987 military coups — agreed to install his erstwhile tormentor as Prime Minister of Fiji.

The arrangement was born not of goodwill but of pure arithmetic.

Rabuka’s Soqosoqo ni Vakavulewa ni Taukei (SVT) had won only 30 of the 37 indigenous Fijian seats in the newly elected 70-seat Parliament, falling short of the outright majority required to govern.

Worse, a fierce internal challenge had emerged.

Josevata Kamikamica — the technocratic former finance minister backed by former prime minister Ratu Sir Kamisese Mara and the traditional eastern chiefly establishment — was threatening to split the indigenous vote on the parliamentary floor.

Kamikamica, to his proponents, was well educated, fiscally disciplined, and a product of the aristocratic civil service elite.

To the establishment, Rabuka was seen as an undisciplined commoner who had disrupted Fiji’s natural social order with his 1987 coups.

Faced with the prospect of losing the premiership to a rival within his own camp, Rabuka turned to the Fiji Labour Party and its 13 Indo-Fijian seats.

In exchange for its floor votes, Labour extracted a written agreement binding Rabuka to four concrete commitments: an immediate and comprehensive review of the discriminatory 1990 Constitution; a review of the Agricultural Landlord and Tenants Act (ALTA) to address expiring land leases; the revocation of anti-labour reforms enacted by the post-coup interim government; and the scrapping of Value Added Tax.

Chaudhry, ever the pragmatist, was asked by journalists why he was backing the man who had deposed him.

“I have noticed changes in him as he becomes more mature.”

It was a characteristically calculated answer.

The deal gave Rabuka his majority and it also gave Chaudhry leverage.

For twelve months, that leverage went largely unused.

Rabuka governed, but the four conditions gathered dust.

By mid-1993, Chaudhry’s patience had visibly expired.

In June of that year, Rabuka delivered a televised address announcing that the Great Council of Chiefs would need to be consulted before any constitutional changes could proceed.

“The process will take time,” Rabuka told the nation, insisting it was “far better and more helpful and productive to be patient.”

In a statement that same week, Chaudhry asked publicly: “Who is running this country — the GCC and the provincial councils, or elected representatives of the people? If so, then why are we going through this democratic charade of a parliamentary process?”

It was a declaration that the arrangement was on life support.

The constitutional question dominated the opening of the new parliamentary session.

President Ratu Sir Penaia Ganilau’s speech to the joint sitting of Parliament on June 21, 1993, sought to reassure, promising the review would be complete in time for the 1997 elections and that a successor to ALTA would be in place by 1995.

But within 24 hours, the deputy speaker had resigned.

Navin Maharaj, who also served as the FLP’s secretary-general, handed his letter to Speaker Dr Apenisa Kurisaqila, saying he could see “definitely clear indications” that the SVT had “no intention of having speedy discussions on a question of a review.”

He revealed that the FLP had written to Rabuka twice seeking a meeting and had received no acknowledgement.

On 23 June 1993, thirteen FLP members of parliament walked out of the chamber.

At a press conference afterwards, Chaudhry was blunt.

“I don’t trust him anymore,” he said of Rabuka.

“We gave him our conditional support, and we are entitled to withdraw that support. The whole process of reviewing the Constitution has been talked about for a year now and nothing has been done yet.”

He went further, accusing the Prime Minister of being controlled by external forces.
